message:
fix(codec): reject oversize tagged_len before accumulation in buffered_frame_len (ft-phz7x)

Close the pre-allocation memory-amplification hole in the streaming
codec. The inner decode_raw already enforces MAX_PDU_SIZE at
lib.rs:395-403 — a frame whose tagged_len declares a payload larger
than MAX_PDU_SIZE is rejected there. But that check fires AFTER the
caller has accumulated the full advertised number of bytes into its
read buffer: stream_decode's pre-buffer check in buffered_frame_len
returns Ok(None) while `buffer.len() < total_len`, which is
try_read_and_decode's "I need more bytes" signal, so a caller would
keep extending the read buffer 4 KiB at a time until it reached the
declared length — even if that length is 10 GiB.

Net effect pre-fix: an attacker who controls the wire stream can
advertise any tagged_len up to u64::MAX, and the client will keep
reading from the socket and growing its buffer until it OOMs the
process or the advertised bytes actually arrive.

## Fix: mirror the inner cap at the outer check

Insert the bounds check in buffered_frame_len immediately after
converting leb128 tagged_len → usize:

    if payload_len > MAX_PDU_SIZE {
        anyhow::bail!(
            "buffered PDU payload size {} exceeds maximum {} — refusing to accumulate",
            payload_len,
            MAX_PDU_SIZE,
        );
    }

This fires BEFORE the caller has a chance to keep accumulating.
stream_decode now surfaces an Err to the caller on the very first
try_read_and_decode invocation that sees the oversize header, so
the attacker's buffer-growing pressure is gone.

The error message explicitly says "refusing to accumulate" so the
caller's log trail distinguishes this pre-allocation rejection from
the existing post-allocation MAX_PDU_SIZE rejection in decode_raw.

## Regression test

`buffered_frame_len_rejects_oversize_tagged_len_ft_phz7x` crafts a
leb128 header advertising `MAX_PDU_SIZE + 1`, feeds it through
buffered_frame_len with a minimal (insufficient) trailing buffer,
and asserts:
  - Err is returned (pre-fix: Ok(None))
  - error message contains both "exceeds maximum" and "refusing to
    accumulate" so the caller's grep target remains stable

Companion sanity check inside the same test: a header advertising
EXACTLY `MAX_PDU_SIZE` (not +1) with an insufficient buffer returns
Ok(None). That's the "legitimate large-but-in-bounds frame, need
more bytes" path. This catches any off-by-one drift in the
comparison direction (`>` vs `>=`) — the boundary-sized frame must
still be considered potentially-valid, not oversize.

The boundary sanity check deliberately does NOT allocate a full
256 MiB buffer — it only drives the length-decode path far enough
to exercise the "buffer.len() < total_len" early-return, which is
all that's needed to verify the comparison direction. Rejecting a
header with a 256 MiB allocation just to pin the direction would
be slow and memory-hostile to CI.

Tracks ft-phz7x.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
